mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the full history of human struggle against disease from miasma through germ theory, epidemic diseases (plague, smallpox, cholera), the development of epidemiology, vaccination, antibiotics, and public health -- spanning noncommunicable and communicable disease, endemic and epidemic disease, and disease control.

Immunisation covers the immune system's mechanisms, active and passive immunisation, vaccine development for multiple diseases, and public health campaigns -- directly relevant to immunity and disease control.

The origins of infectious disease covers how pathogens co-evolved with hosts, zoonotic transmission, crowd diseases, antibiotic resistance, and emerging infections -- directly relevant to the multifactorial concept of contagious disease and endemic/epidemic patterns.

Parasitism covers the diversity of parasitic organisms causing disease (viruses, bacteria, protozoa, helminths), their strategies for immune evasion, and the disease burden on humans and livestock -- directly relevant to communicable disease in animals.
Louis Pasteur's germ theory of disease, vaccines for rabies and anthrax, and disproof of spontaneous generation are foundational to understanding communicable disease and its control.
